data.title: AutoNation: Weak Balance Sheet Under The Strong Sales
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Tariff barriers have made used cars more expensive and are selling a little faster than otherwise in America. AutoNation (NYSE:AN) benefits from this trend in its sales. But the underlying strength of its sales from various metrics shows weakness and vulnerability lurking. Added with its weak free cash flow and high debt load, the company's high valuation isn't justified.

Company Overview

Autonation, founded in 1996 with headquarters in Fort Lauderdale, Florida, is one of the largest automotive retailers in the United States with 325 new vehicle franchises from 243 stores.

Analysis

AutoNation reported strong sales in the first quarter of 2025 with both its top line and bottom line beating the consensus. However, these upbeat results masked an underlying declining trend in the gross PVR (Price Per Vehicle Retailed). For the Used Car, the gross PVR is at $1,662, about 6.68% higher than the annual average of 2024, but its New Car gross PVR is $2,803, an 8% lower in a similar comparison. The New Car sales are the largest from its revenue, which we will analyze in detail later. Overall, both are still significantly off from their peak, but still above the pre-COVID era.

Furthermore, the margins for both New and Used cars seem to have continued to trend lower. Although the Used Cars have a slight pickup in Q1 at 6.3% from 5.9% previous year's average, it doesn't escape a long-term falling trend since 2008. The New Car margin declined from 5.9% average in 2024 to Q1 '25 at 5.4%. In its Q1 presentation, the company gave a bullish FY 2025 average expectation of the margins for both margins, but it seems to hard to reconcile for us. It expects the FY25's gross PVR for New Car to be $2,611, lower than the Q1 number, but FY margin to rise to 6.6% from Q1's 5.4%. Similarly, it expects Used Car's gross PVR to be $1,617, also lower than Q1, but the FY average margin for these cars to be 8%. We think both margins could be on a continuous decline should the expected FY average gross PVR's be realized.

The revenue mix shows AutoNation is still predominantly reliant on New Car for sales, which contributes 48.8% of the sales but only 16.2% of gross profit. This makes New Cars sales' relative profitability at only 33%, and Used Cars sales are about 31%. This means the company has to make 3 units of New Car sales to contribute to 1 unit of gross profit, similarly for Used Cars. But Used Cars got this level of relative profitability with just a bit more than half of New Car sales. This level of margin is trailing far behind the other two segments. Parts and Service is contributing 46.2% of the gross profit with only 17.2% of the sales, not to mention that Finance and Insurance's outsized profit contribution of 28.4% with only 5.1% of the sales. That means Parts and Services is relatively profitable at 2.7x and Financing is 5.56x. Overall, its largest sales component is the least profitable one.

The divergence in profit margins we show above and the declining trend in gross PVR explain the difference between its revenue and gross profit. With a stable to slightly growing revenue, AutoNation's gross margin has been on a steep decline since 2022. The question is, at what level will its gross profit hold? The average gross margin pre-COVID is around 16.8% and the lowest it has been in the past 20 years is at about 15%; it is currently slightly below 18%. Its gross margin can only decline another 100bps to keep the growth momentum, and in about another 250bps to slip into a bearish outlook.

In addition to the sliding gross profit, AutoNation also has weaker free cash flow, measured both in relation to its EBITDA and Revenue. The company's free cash flow to EBITDA ratio has been negative since mid-last year, and now only falling further to about -25%. Its free cash flow to revenue ratio is also squarely in red at about -1%.

AutoNation's debt load has seen increases in both long term and short term debt. Its earnings were greatly impacted in 2020 but recovered quickly afterwards. As a result, its debt-to-EBITDA ratio stayed around 2x for about two years in 2022-2023 until consistently trending up to close 5.8x now. Its debt-to-equity ratio shows a less distorted picture since its equity is more stable than earnings. Currently, debt-to-equity for the company is at around 3.7x. It has been at such a high level before in 2016 and 2023. From 2016 onward, this ratio was reduced substantially to 1.5x in about 5 years. So the current rise in debt-to-equity ratio is a new trend in the past year and a half.

The recent rise in used car sales has thrust car dealers back into the spotlight, following their previous boom time during the COVID pandemic. Although the determination of the White House to implement the tariff could have lasting impact on the national car sales, the actual trend since last year is much more gentle than the one in 2020-21. If you blink twice, you might even see it as almost flat.

Industry-wise, there are several trends that are upending the traditional retail car sales model, such as online retailers, digitization of car sales and after-sales services, also the mobility-as-service trend from ride-hailing companies will continue to change the trend of sales. The need to upgrade and invest in CapEx will only increase. The higher debt ratio and negative free cash flow will hinder that growth for AutoNation's competitiveness. We think that investors being laser-focused on AutoNation's sales because of the latest market trend is a misnomer. From a big picture point of view, we have a more neutral view on the company growth perspective. It is best that AutoNation uses its current sales strength to pay down its debt and repair its negative free cash flow, which is at one of its lowest levels in the past 15 years. So when the music stops for the used car sale boom, it won't be caught swimming naked.

Financial Overview & Valuation

Currently, AutoNation's EV-to-EBITDA ratio at 10.93x is the middle range of its peers, such as Lithia Motors (LAD), Group 1 Automotive(GPI), and Asbury Automotive(ABG), but it is trending up for the past year or so a little faster than GPI and ABG due to the declining speed of its EBITDA during this time.

However, by the price-to-book ratio, AutoNation is the most expensive. The book value of its equity is eroded by the high debt load. To buy the company at the current price will be paying 3.38x of the total shareholder equity, while most of its competitors are at 1.3-2x. We don't think it is justified, given the declining margin and negative cash flow. We would recommend a Hold for its stock at the current price or higher.

Risks to the Thesis

The risk to the thesis is on the upside. Suppose that the tariff deters many more cars from being imported into the country in the next 12-18 months, then AutoNation could continue to ride the rising tide for a while. And as we have shown, sales don't translate fully into gross profit. AutoNation doesn't have a lot of room to avoid fixing its negative cash flow and high debt. On the other hand, making these efforts could lead to lower earnings in the short term. Either way, its valuation won't become cheap.

Earnings Preview

For the upcoming earnings release, it is likely AutoNation continues to have strong sales of New and Used cars, as the tariff zigzag that happened in Q2 spurred more consumers to make that delayed purchase decision. Its revenue could still beat consensus, and the earnings could either be right on target or slightly better. However, we expect margin and gross PVR to continue to trend lower. It would be interesting to see where its gross profits are. Additionally, strong sales in Q2 could also lay the ground for a fall later in the year as the consumers' demand is pulled ahead due to tariff concerns when inflation really starts to bite. In any case, we see the current boom time as a limited window for the company to address the medium-term problems. In other words, if it's a beat, we would recommend fading it instead of chasing the highs.

Conclusion

Contrasting AutoNation's weak balance sheet with its strong sales, we draw attention to some of it underlying weaknesses of the retailer. Instead of parsing its car sales by gender or age, we think its margin, cash flow and debt load are more important issues to consider. We think its valuation is on the rich side and recommend a hold.

data.title: QuantumScape Corporation: Q2 2025 Earnings Preview
data.description:
Introduction

Recently, the EV value chain is under immense pressure from raw material volatility and tighter capital availability. The traditional lithium-ion companies are ramping up energy density making QuantumScape Corporation (NYSE:QS) breakthrough an heated debate. QS, a leader in the next generation solid state lithium-metal batteries is expected to report its Q2 2025 earnings report on 23rd,July 2025. This report is significant because it might signal if its solid state is finding traction or whether investors’ patience should wear thin.

With the EV adoption accelerating and battery technology becoming a key differentiator, this company’s progress in commercializing its high-energy-density batteries which are fast charging is a point of interest for many investors.

Looking at the 93.64% stock surge YTD beating the S&P 500 6.58% return, I think investor optimism is palpable potentially fueled by recent breakthroughs such as the Cobra separator process.

Nevertheless, the company is still pre-revenue and its profitability path remains speculative. Can QS sustain its current momentum and deliver on its ambitious roadmap?

Consensus Expectations And What To Watch In The Upcoming Report

This being a pre-revenue company, I think its earning focus is not majorly on the traditional financial metrics but operational milestones. The consensus EPS is -$0.2 which is in line with previous quarters with no significant revenue due to the company’s pre-commercialization status.

In the recent past, the company has had mixed results with two EPS misses, one beating and one being in line with estimates. It is important to note that since this is a prerevenue company, its EPS is highly influenced by R&D expenses and this draws my attention to its Cobra separator process which has entered baseline production. At this stage, I expect higher expenses as scaling up comes at a cost and for this reason, I anticipate an EPS miss in Q2.

Contexualizing Q2 2024, its R&D expenses came in at $97.7 million up from $86.5 million YoY and capital expenditure was approximately $18.9 million. This was due its QSE-5 prototype production and Cobra process. From this perspective, I believe scaling up Cobra separator production will have a similar effect pushing up expenses. I am expecting an earnings loss per share between -$0.23 and $0.25.

Nevertheless, QS being yet to register significant financials, the most important aspects in its coming report are not the financial metrics but its milestones towards the commercialization goal. For this reason. The following are the two major areas I think we should pay attention to in this coming quarterly release.

  • Battery performance updates: We should pay a close attention to potential updates on its solid state lithium metal battery developments, particularly the advancement in Cobra separator process. Recently, it was integrated into standard battery production boosting production speed by ~25x and reducing equipment footprint thus paving way for scaled production. This was a significant milestone which saw its stock soar by about 30%. Any positive updates from this end would signal progress towards commercialization and that would lead to positive sentiments sustaining the current momentum.
  • Production and scalability milestones: Investors should pay attention to any potential insights into the timeline and success of the QSE-5 battery production and Cobra’s process readiness which was achieved ahead of schedule. We should focus on CapEx updates and potential partnerships such as the collaboration with PowerCo which includes a $130 million prepayment for licensing. Any meaningful developments in scalability would signify a step closer to commercialization while new partnerships would signify stronger market traction. This is because to me it would be a validation of its technology and market acceptance.

The Bullish Perspective: Looking Beyond Q2 Earnings

While the upcoming quarterly performance is a short-term price catalyst, I think the long-term view matters alongside the anticipated release. I will begin by reflecting on the superior features of this company’s product which would serve as its value proposition in my view. The company’s solid-state lithium metal batteries offer superior performance as seen through different metrics.

To begin with, its anode free design has a density of 844 Wh/L which is significantly higher than conventional lithium ion batteries which is the region of 300Wh/L. Further, it supports fast charging reactions hitting 80% capacity in under 15 minutes as well as retaining 95% capacity after 1,000 cycles equivalent to about 500,000 km of driving. Above all, its ability to operate at temperatures as low as -30 degrees Celsius and the use of noncombustible and nonflammable separator enhance safety by reducing fire risks.

I don’t just look at these features in isolation, I look at them against the market gap because that speaks about the product’s relevance in the current EV market. Looking at the above features, they seem to address two major gaps in the current market. The first gap is limited range and range anxiety. With current consumer concerned about range anxiety and shorter driving ranges, this company’s 844Wh/L being higher than the conventional battery offers a solution to this making it a relevant product in the market.

Secondly, the other gap is slow charging times. With a typical EV car taking about 8 hours to charge from empty to full using a 7kW charging point, and many electric cars taking about 35 minutes to add about 100 miles of range with a 50kW rapid charger, it is evident that this is a major gap which results to inconveniences. Given this company’s fast charging battery in about 15 minutes with a 95% retention after significant millage, I see its product as a major game changer in this industry.

I am looking at the market opportunity here given its superior product. With the global Solid-State battery market projected to grow by a CAGR of 49.4% between 2025 and 2032, the opportunity is vast for this company. With this company developing the first anodeless cell design with superior features underscores its market leadership and positions it better to seize the opportunities in this growing market.

It is estimated that the global EV adoption will rise significantly in the coming years with approximately 40% the global auto market expected to be made of EVs by 2030. Considering that this company’s technology addresses two main gaps (slow charging and limited range) in the EV industry, its market appeal is significant once it commercializes it product.

The Downside

While the Solid battery seems to be the game changer in this industry, QS faces competition from giants like Toyota and Samsung. With Samsung aiming to commercialize its solid battery by 2027, it threatens to erode QS first mover advantage. Of course QS being a startup faces execution risk as scaling from lab to gigafactory is capital intensive. In the absence of concrete OEM contracts QS could be faced with financial challenges hence limited market penetration. However, with its innovative product, I think it’s very likely to win major contracts.

Final Thoughts

Although the long-term outlook is positive, the upcoming quarterly report is very critical because it could impact the market sentiments sigficantly. Currently, this stock is on strong upward momentum.

Should the Q2 report turn out to be strong, I expect this momentum to be sustained. Recent gains such as the 30% surge following Cobra Separator process progress is an indication of how the market is responsive to the company’s milestone. This shows why any major announcements in the Q2 report could move the market significantly. With a beta of almost 2, investors should brace for volatility following quarterly release. From a long-term perspective, I think this company’s innovation is very relevant in the dynamic and growing EV industry. As such, I would recommend buying this stock in dips and holding in the long run.

Given the company’s recent breakthrough with Cobra separator process which was achieved earlier than anticipated, I believe the company is on track to send its QSE-5 B1 samples this year to its customers for field testing next year in line with their plan.

In conclusion, QS is on the right path as hinted by its recent technological breakthrough which has sparked market optimism, although I expect its EPS to fall below expectations due high costs associated with ramping up production, I anticipate positive updates on key milestone if the current trajectory is anything to go by. After the earnings report, I will offer a comprehensive follow-up coverage on the stock where I will dive deeper into what the report shall contain and what it implies to the company and us as investors.

data.title: Orla Mining: A Transformational Start To The Year
data.description:
All figures are in United States Dollars unless otherwise noted. G/T = grams per tonne (of gold or silver). GEOs = gold-equivalent ounces. SEOs = silver-equivalent ounces. AISC refers to all-in sustaining costs. LOMP = life of mine plan. TPD = tonnes per day. UG = Underground. MTPA = million tonnes per annum. FS/DFS = Definitive Feasibility Study.

Q1 Production & Sales

Orla Mining released its Q1-25 results, reporting record quarterly production of ~47,800 ounces of gold and sales of ~46,400 ounces of gold. This represented a 44% increase in output from the year-ago period, helped by another solid quarter out of Camino Rojo (~30,000 ounces produced at $845/oz AISC), and partial contribution from its new Musselwhite Mine in Ontario. The latter closed at the end of February and produced ~17,800 ounces in March, with ~104,300 tonnes processed at 5.55 G/T of gold.

Importantly, and unlike several producers that have blown out their share counts to acquire assets in this cycle, Orla added this mid-scale Canadian gold asset with no upfront share dilution and modest dilution from warrants/convertible debt (~18% dilution), translating to meaningful growth in per share metrics. In fact, production per share was up nearly 50% in just a partial quarter of contribution from Musselwhite, with production per share set to more than double in 2025, with further growth in 2026 from a full year of production from Musselwhite at planned higher grades (6.8+ G/T of gold).

Hence, this has been a transformational start to the year for Orla, to say the least. Plus, these production per share metrics do not include the South Carlin Complex, an above-average grade heap leach asset with immense exploration potential (oxide and sulfide) along the highly productive Carlin Trend, home to a ~1.2 million ounce gold producer, the Carlin Complex, with a ~34 million ounce resource base. This is not meant to suggest that Orla is sitting on any 10+ million ounce elephant-scale deposits at its South Carlin Complex, but it's certainly in the right neighborhood for key discoveries and there's evidence of high-grade and high-margin oxide material here (with historic hits like ~126.2 meters at 3.95 G/T of gold), and an abundance of low to mid-grade oxide material that should be able to easily support a 15-year mine life vs. the ~8 year LOMP currently.

As for Orla's progress vs. annual guidance, the company has produced ~125,600 ounces year-to-date, or 43.3% of planned production, with what should be two ~80,000 ounce production quarters on deck to help it reach its 280,000 to 300,000 ounce guidance. And if history is any guide, Orla will deliver on this guidance and come in closer to 300,000 ounces for the year, with an incredible track record to date of beating its guidance midpoint, being one of the few companies that has a habit of massively over-delivering on promises.

Financial Results

Moving to its financial results, Orla reported quarterly revenue of ~$140.7 million (+109% year-over-year) and solid cash flow generation, with the company set to generate upwards of $200 million in free cash flow this year. This is despite significant investments at Musselwhite (~$108 million in sustaining capital and capitalized exploration), and an aggressive exploration program across the portfolio, with ~$43 million in exploration/development expenses across its three sites.

As for Orla stacks up vs. peers, we can see that it's been one of the most consistent companies in its junior/mid-tier peer group from a free cash flow generation standpoint despite being one of the smallest (just over 100,000 ounces/year in the 2021-2024 period), but free cash flow will take a material step higher in 2025, while free cash flow per share should also hit record highs above US$0.53/share on a diluted basis. This is important because while there's no shortage of producers reporting record headline results, their per share figures leave a lot to be desired in most cases, with Coeur Mining (CDE) being one clear example where we've seen free cash flow per share tumble over 80% since 2011 levels even in a record year for gold prices.

In terms of Orla's balance sheet, it's shifted from a strong net cash position (Q4-24: ~$160 million in net cash), to a net debt position, with over $260 million in net debt as of the end of Q1-25. This is related to the purchase of the Musselwhite acquisition, with a fully drawn $250 million credit facility ($100 million term loan, $150 million under existing RCF), and $200 million in unsecured convertible debt (4.5%, maturing in 2030), or $450 million in new debt against its ~$184 million cash position. Fortunately, the gold price has been working in Orla's favor, and the company will be able to quickly de-lever and should exit 2026 back in a net cash position, even under more conservative gold price assumptions.

Funding of the Musselwhite transaction also included a $360 million gold pre-pay on gold forward prices averaging $2,834/oz, with 36 equal monthly deliveries from March 2025 to February 2028 ~4,025 ounces of gold.

Costs & Margins

Looking at Orla's costs and margins, Orla reported Q1-25 AISC of $845/oz based on its Camino Rojo Mine only (does not include Musselwhite), a 7% decline from the year-ago period. The improvement in AISC was largely related to significantly lower sustaining capital spend in the period (~$0.5 million vs. ~$5.0 million) and a much weaker Mexican Peso, partially offset by fewer ounces of gold sold at Camino Rojo. Lower sustaining capital was related to easy comps vs. the higher sustaining capital in Q1-24 related to the construction of Phase 2 of its heap leach pad and higher capitalized exploration.

Overall, these costs were in line with its revised guidance midpoint of $850/oz (Camino Rojo only), benefiting from the weaker Mexican Peso and lower sustaining capital, with just $0.5 million of a planned $5.0 million (revised down from $10.0 million) spent in Q1-25. As for margins, AISC margins surged to $2,070/oz (71.0%), up from $1,150/oz in the year-ago period. This was primarily related to its record average realized gold price of $2,915/oz, in addition to abnormally low sustaining capital in Q1-25 that further benefited margins.

That said, we will see a move higher in AISC starting in Q2-25 at Camino Rojo, impacted by higher royalties (gold-price related), the sharp reversal in the Mexican Peso which has strengthened in Q2-25, and catch-up spend in sustaining capital. As for company-wide AISC, I wouldn't be surprised to see it come in above $1,425/oz in Q2-25, with this obviously largely because of the higher-cost Musselwhite operation where costs have been guided for $1,650/oz at the mid-point in the April to December period. Looking at the chart below, this will de-throne Orla as one of the sector's lowest-cost producers, with full-year 2025 AISC likely to come in above $1,400/oz.

While the higher cost profile that places it closer to the industry average (~$1,600/oz) may turn off some investors, it's important to point out that this deal is truly transformational and a brilliant move by the company even if it has pushed its all-in sustaining costs over 65% higher (~$850/oz ---> $1,400/oz+). This is because it gives Orla scale and it de-risks the company with improved diversification from a net asset value and cash flow standpoint. Plus, it paves a path to being a tri-asset producer in 2028, with less focus on a jurisdiction that has lost some ground in terms of investment attractiveness (Mexico), and the bulk of production/cash flow coming from Tier-1 ranked jurisdictions (Ontario, Canada and Nevada, USA).

Most importantly, and as we'll dig into below, Orla paid a very reasonable price for this asset ($850 million including contingent consideration), given that this is an already producing asset with just shy of 2.0 million meters drilled on the property, a 1.5 MTPA plant, a twin decline and newly commissioned conveyor/material handling system under Newmont's (NEM) ownership, all on a ~65,000 hectare land package north of Thunder Bay, Ontario.

As evidence (*) of the very reasonable price paid, Musselwhite was acquired for an estimated NAV of $1.0 billion (5% discount rate, $2,500/oz gold price) or 0.85x P/NAV, with that NAV having room to increase materially given that gold has averaged over $3,200/oz since the deal closed and $2,500/oz could end up being a conservative long-term gold price assumption.

(*) Comparing this to other acquisitions, we saw De Grey Mining (OTCPK:DGMLF) acquired for over 0.80x P/NAV (~$2.72 billion price tag) as a development-stage asset with what's likely to be at least $1.5 billion in capex to move into full production, while Spartan Resources was acquired for over 1.0x P/NAV (~$1.56 billion price tag), also as a development-stage asset, albeit with an existing 2.5 MTPA plant. (*)

To summarize, investors would be best to ignore the upcoming increase in costs as a result of this transaction and focus on the big picture, which is the creation of a new mid-tier and Tier-1 focused gold producer under exceptional management and with two assets that have received nowhere near the exploration dollars that they deserve over the past decade, with both in prolific jurisdictions (Northern Ontario, Carlin Trend).

Recent Developments

Musselwhite Acquisition

Digging into the Musselwhite acquisition, Orla has added a ~200,000 ounce per annum producing mine in Canada with a mine life out to 2030 and what should be sub $1,400/oz all-in sustaining costs on average over the next few years adjusting for inflation. This was achieved with modest debt ($450 million), very modest share dilution (up to 18% in future dilution from warrants/notes) and the company paid ~$472/oz on M&I resources or ~$427/oz on total resource ounces for this producing asset based on its ~1.5 million ounce reserve base and ~2.3 million ounce total resource base.

Looking at the above chart, Musselwhite sees a step up in production in 2026 based on higher grades to ~240,000 ounces, pushing Orla's production profile closer to 350,000 ounces next year. However, non-core assets like Musselwhite in a major's portfolio typically see the proverbial scraps from an investment dollar standpoint, with large-scale producers understandably the most focused on their Tier-1 scale assets (400,000+ ounces/year). Hence, there looks to be significant room to optimize this asset and extend the mine life, with Newmont more focused on its major growth projects (Tanami, Ahafo North, Cerro Negro Expansion), and hunting down and subsequently integrating its large-scale gold-copper assets and Lihir over the past few years vs. investing in its smaller Ontario mines picked up in the Goldcorp acquisition.

It's important to note, though, that while Musselwhite operated at ~1.03 million tonnes in 2024 (212,000 ounces at $1,541/oz AISC) under Newmont, it has a plant capable of processing ~1.5 million tonnes per annum and regularly operated at 1.3 - 1.5 MTPA levels under Goldcorp. Leveraging this excess capacity could provide an opportunity to improve operating costs and push production back towards 300,000 ounces per annum. Further, this is an asset with a solid track record of reserve replacement, with it having ~2.12 million ounces at 6.24 G/T of gold as of year-end 2010 under Goldcorp and still having ~1.53 million ounces of gold at 6.43 G/T of gold in reserves today after production of over 2.5 million ounces of gold.

In terms of the bigger potential for this asset, Orla appears quite confident it can drive down costs, with it hoping to reduce mining dilution, optimize haulage and increase mine productivity and upgrade its cemented rock fill system. Further, the recently completed Technical Report noted that mill spending on contractors, technical services and maintenance was above average for a plant of this size, suggesting the potential for additional cost savings. In fact, Orla has guided aggressively in terms of this asset's potential, suggesting it expects to produce "more than 300,000 ounces of gold at a combined all-in cost of about $1,100 per ounce."

Moving to exploration, Orla believes that there's the potential for additional mineralization up to 1 kilometer beyond current reserves in the area of the PD Deep Extension[s]. The company plans to test this with surface directional drilling to confirm the continuity of the deposit down plunge, with there also being the opportunity to explore its larger mining lease for new opportunities to potentially fill the mill. The company had the following to say:

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

"....we expect to achieve multiple years of life of mine extension through ongoing exploration."

"And then looking into the future and speaking to that additional 2 kilometers of strike that we will attack next to find the continuation of that banded formation. And as it continues, we believe that there will be an additional 1.0 million ounces there that could support an expansion of the Musselwhite asset to a size that it has never been. And clearly, the capital and design for such an expansion, particularly material handling to fill the mill, is something that we look forward to studying over the next couple of years."

- Orla Mining M&A Call

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Assuming Orla is right on this potential to add 1.0 million ounces and extend production into the mid 2030s or further from its current ~6-year mine life, this would make this acquisition a home-run and allow Orla to sustain a 500,000 ounce production profile well into next decade as production levels taper off at its South Carlin Complex (peak production of ~195,000 ounces, but average production closer to ~120,000 ounces/year). It would also result in a meaningful boost to net asset value per share, with it clearly set to be a very exciting 18 months for investors as we get more detail and visibility into actual exploration upside and the longer-term potential for this asset post-optimization.

South Carlin Complex

Finally, in terms of its South Carlin Complex, Orla hopes to have its Record of Decision in mid-2026, paving a path to first production by the end of 2027. This asset is expected to add ~194,000 ounces on average in Year 2 and 3, dropping to ~107,000 ounces in Year 4 through Year 7 per its dated LOMP completed in February 2022 by Gold Standard. Assuming Orla receives its ROD by Q4-26, we should see commercial production by late 2028, setting the company up to produce ~530,000 ounces in 2029, assuming an improved production profile from Musselwhite through optimization work.

If achieved, Orla has the potential to increase free cash flow per share to ~$600 million in 2029 (peak year for South Carlin Complex) or US$1.55/share, nearly tripling from FY2025 levels, with this based on a conservative $2,800/oz gold price assumption, and upwards of $660 million (US$1.70/share) in free cash flow at a $3,000/oz gold price assumption in 2029. Hence, there is still material upside here for patient investors, assuming Musselwhite can deliver as expected (maintain 250,000+ ounces/year and extend its mine life) and South Railroad operates as planned, with this asset having low technical risk as a relatively simple oxide heap-leach operation.

Let's dig into the valuation and see how much of this is priced into the stock today.

Valuation

Based on ~390 million fully diluted shares and a share price of US$11.85, Orla trades at a market cap of ~$4.62 billion and an enterprise value of ~$4.89 billion. This leaves Orla as one of the highest market cap producers in the sub 400,000 ounce producer space, sitting at an ~80% higher enterprise value than Torex Gold (OTCPK:TORXF) with 400,000+ GEOs of annual production, and at a similar enterprise value to B2Gold (BTG), a ~1.0 million ounce producer.

In defense of Orla's premium valuation, the company has a better jurisdictional tilt than some of its peers from a NAV standpoint (Canada, Mexico, United States), a superior margin profile than the industry average, and most importantly, a team with significant experience that has done an incredible job of creating shareholder value to date coupled with near flawless operational execution. This obviously deserves a premium multiple, but I would argue that a decent chunk of this growth and future execution is priced into the stock today at a ~$4.9 billion enterprise value.

So, what's a fair value for the stock?

Using what I believe to be fair multiples of 10x FY2026 cash flow estimates and 1.1x P/NAV (7%) for what is now a primarily Tier-1 jurisdiction gold producer with improved diversification, a strong pipeline (South Carlin Complex, Camino Rojo Sulphides) and a glowing track record of operational execution/per share growth, I see an updated fair value for ORLA of US$9.95. And while this is a significant increase from my prior fair value estimate of US$5.40 (pre-acquisition), this still leaves Orla fully valued today.

Obviously, valuation alone does not dictate where stock prices go short term, and it's quite possible that ORLA could continue to climb higher with an enviable new portfolio following the Musselwhite acquisition. Further, my fair value assumption uses a $3,050/oz gold price in 2026 for conservatism. That said, looking at the stock today as an investment, I don't see enough margin of safety at current levels to justify paying up for the stock above US$11.80 here with some of this future growth already priced in.

As for the bigger picture, Orla is certainly unique among its mid-tier peers with the potential to add another 200,000 ounces of above-average margin Tier-1 gold exposure from Nevada in H2-28 during peak years of production from its South Carlin Complex. Looking out to 2029 in peak production years for South Railroad (Year 2/3 average production of ~200,000 ounces), Orla has the potential to produce ~525,000 ounces company-wide, translating to ~$660 million in free cash flow at a $3,000/oz gold price assumption. And even if we put a conservative 12x multiple on that potential, Orla's fair value jumps to ~US$20.50/share on ~385 million shares.

Hence, if one is willing to look out a few years, there is clear long-term upside here. The problem I see is that the stock looks close to fully valued short term and is still three years away from a 500,000 ounce production profile, assuming a more conservative start to commercial production of Q1-28 for its South Carlin Complex.

In summary, while ORLA has a very bright future with relatively low risk given the jurisdictional upgrade and technical strength of this team, the best course of action looks to be waiting for sharp pullbacks to add to positions vs. rushing into the stock here above US$11.80.

Summary

Orla reported an impressive quarter in Q1-25 with costs well below the industry average, but we will see a material step higher in costs as we see a full quarter of contribution from its new Musselwhite Mine in Q2-25 (not included in Q2-25 AISC). However, Orla will still be a below average cost producer, it has undergone a complete transformation, and Musselwhite has given it 500,000+ ounce potential longer-term, with further upside if it develops Camino Rojo Sulphides (polymetallic opportunity in Mexico) at the end of this decade.

Importantly, this material increase in reserves, production and clear upgrade from a jurisdictional standpoint (largest asset now in Ontario, Canada) was achieved with modest dilution and very manageable debt levels and has translated to industry-leading growth in per share metrics. Hence, with an impressive growth profile, increased scale and better diversification, Orla is a far lower-risk business today than it was 12 months ago. All that being said, the stock has seen a significant move off its 2022 lows, is now trading at a ~$4.9 billion enterprise value or over ~$16,500 per ounce on an EV/2025 production basis, and remains extended short-term from a technical standpoint.

So, while I see ORLA as a top-10 growth story sector-wide led by a team with a clear ability to create shareholder value and deliver on promises, I don't see any way to justify chasing the stock up here at US$11.80 with no real margin of safety at current levels.

data.title: The Only Time I Would Buy VYM Instead Of SCHD
data.description:
I recently wrote about why the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) is my favorite dividend growth ETF right now. In particular, it has a low expense ratio, a relatively attractive dividend yield, a very impressive dividend growth track record, and a diversified portfolio of blue-chip dividend growth stocks. While there is certainly a lot to like about it, there are other good alternatives out there, too. One of these is the Vanguard High Dividend Yield Index Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:VYM). In this article, I will detail why it is a worthy competitor to SCHD and share the only time I would buy VYM instead of SCHD.

Why VYM ETF Could Be A Better Option Than SCHD

The bottom line up front is that I would buy VYM instead of SCHD if I were looking for a one-stop-shop dividend growth ETF to buy. The reason for this is pretty straightforward, namely that it has the same very low expense ratio of 0.06%, so it is one of the most efficient ETFs out there. However, it has far more positions than SCHD and is much better diversified. For example, it has 591 individual holdings compared to SCHD's 103 holdings. Additionally, it has only 25% of its portfolio allocated across its top 10 holdings, whereas SCHD has a whopping 40.3% of its portfolio allocated across its top 10 holdings. This makes SCHD a pretty concentrated bet on a handful of companies, whereas VYM is much more strategically spread across many other holdings. At the same time, however, VYM does have a 5.83% stake in Broadcom (AVGO), which gives it significant exposure to the AI boom while still being invested in a powerful dividend grower. In contrast, SCHD lacks nearly the same exposure to leading tech stocks that VYM does.

This brings me to my next point: VYM has much better balanced diversification across sectors, whereas SCHD has recently become increasingly a heavy bet on energy, with nearly 20% of its portfolio invested in the sector. In contrast, VYM has a much more reasonable 8.3% allocated to this volatile sector. Instead, it has greater exposure to sectors like financials and technology stocks, whereas SCHD has much less exposure to financials and technology stocks. Additionally, SCHD hardly has any exposure to utilities, while VYM has 6.04% invested in utilities. This makes VYM better diversified to include more tech as well as infrastructure-related stocks while leaning less aggressively into the more volatile energy sector.

As such, VYM is better positioned to be a standalone fund, whereas SCHD is best used when supplemented by exposure to infrastructure and even some megacap tech stocks, perhaps through funds like the Reeves Utility Income Trust (UTG) and JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium ETF (JEPQ) for investors who want yield from their megacap tech exposure. Or, if they do not need that extra yield and simply want to maximize total returns, the Schwab U.S. Large Cap Growth ETF (SCHG) is a nice pair with SCHD. This better diversification of VYM and, in particular, its greater exposure to megacap tech has shown in its significant outperformance of SCHD in recent years.

When SCHD Is The Better Choice

That being said, SCHD does have some strengths relative to VYM. In particular, it has a much higher current dividend yield of nearly 120 basis points. Additionally, it has a vastly superior dividend growth track record with a nearly 11% dividend CAGR over the past 10 years, in contrast to VYM's 5.56% dividend CAGR in the past decade. This reflects in SCHD's vastly superior dividend growth over the period during which they both have been active.

In addition, SCHD has delivered superior long-term total returns to VYM, though the two have been closely correlated and VYM has closed the gap considerably in recent years.

Investor Takeaway

What this comparison comes down to, in the end, is that both ETFs are quality, low-cost dividend growth funds that are filled with a diverse set of blue-chip dividend growth stocks. They also both offer dividend yields that are significantly greater than can be found in the broader S&P 500. That being said, VYM is a better pick if you value broad diversification in order to minimize risk and to enjoy more of a one-stop-shop experience with greater exposure to AI tech names as well as utilities. Meanwhile, SCHD is better if you simply want to maximize current yield and dividend growth potential over time and you plan to supplement SCHD with other funds or individual stocks in order to round out the portfolio with better diversification.

Both of these are worthy approaches, especially for investors who want to take a more passive approach to managing their portfolios. My personal view, however, is that I think that buying individual dividend stocks and overlaying an opportunistic capital recycling program is the best approach for maximizing current yield, dividend growth, and total returns over the long term. This is the approach that we take at High Yield Investor.

data.title: URNM: Access To The Uranium/Nuclear Thematic Comes With Complications
data.description:
Introduction

Readers familiar with my work will know that I typically write about value ideas, built on bottom-up, fundamental research. But occasionally I come across a strong investment thematic that prompts me to move out of my comfort zone and build an investment case using a top-down, bigger picture approach. The research that I have done on the psychedelics sector was triggered by top-down thinking, although notes such as my most recent publication on Cybin (CYBN) do get into the weeds at an individual stock level.

I’ve been following the uranium/nuclear story for a couple of years. Initially, I was drawn to NuScale Power Corporation (SMR), as a play on an expectation of increased demand for new nuclear reactors. However, NuScale’s share price today is well above the level at which I considered taking a position. So, I started to think about the uranium sector as an alternative. I’ve worked alongside research analysts covering resources/commodities for long enough to realize that bottom-up, fundamental research on mining stocks is a tough gig, and not something to be taken on without deep knowledge of the relevant commodity market(s). I decided to look for a vehicle that would provide exposure to the sector without requiring me to spend weeks getting up to speed on uranium supply-demand dynamics and that would avoid the need to build DCF models of individual mines.

Sprott Uranium Miners ETF

The Sprott Uranium Miners ETF (NYSEARCA:URNM) struck me as a potential solution to my knowledge and time limitations. Sprott is a specialist asset manager focused on precious metals and critical materials, so investing in URNM would allow me to leverage expert knowledge on the uranium market that I lack. Sprott also claim to be a contrarian investor with a long-term investment horizon – this aligns perfectly with my preferred style of investing.

A review of Seeking Alpha analyst ratings on URNM confirmed that I had jumped on a pretty popular idea, but it also triggered a nagging sense of worry that a bullish view on URNM would be a long way from a contrarian stance to take. My screen showed 41 ratings dating back to late 2019. Of these 41, about 25% were Strong Buy, less than 10% were Hold, and the rest were all Buy ratings. URNM has not attracted a single Sell rating in over five years of coverage by analysts on Seeking Alpha.

In this note I’m going to explain why I’m lacking the enthusiasm to get on board with the URNM bulls, and why I think investors should consider alternative ways to play the investment uranium/nuclear thematic.

Key Arguments Supporting Investment In Uranium Stocks

Much has been written regarding the case for investing in uranium related stocks, and many analysts on Seeking Alpha have done a good job at setting out the bull case. Here’s a quick summary of what I find to be the most salient arguments for taking on uranium exposure.

  • Decarbonization will require more nuclear power. Although renewable energy sources continue to expand, growth in electricity demand implies that other clean sources of energy will be required in order to work toward net-zero ambitions. Governments worldwide are looking more favorably upon nuclear energy as realization grows that solar and wind may be insufficient to solve the problem.
  • The Russia-Ukraine conflict highlights the value of nuclear power as a secure, domestic, energy source. This will influence governments to extend the life of existing reactors and plan new nuclear facilities.
  • Uranium supply is not sufficient to meet the future demand. According to Sprott, even at peak historical production levels, existing and committed uranium mines are not expected to meet reactor demand through 2040, with a nearly 1.3-billion-pound deficit to 2045.
  • Secondary supply sources from decommissioned nuclear weapons have run dry, and utilities are expected to seek longer term primary supply contracts from uranium miners.

Fees On Fees

One of URNM’s largest holdings is a ~11% exposure to the Sprott Physical Uranium Trust (‘SPUT’). Sprott say that SPUT is the world’s largest physical uranium fund. SPUT provides investors with the ability to invest directly in uranium U308, the physical storage of which is handled by Cameco (Canada), ConverDyn (US) and Orana (France). As of June 30, 2025, SPUT held ~$5.3bn of U308.

I have no particular issue with SPUT as an investment vehicle. However, what bothers me is that Sprott are investing 11% of URNM in another fund that they run and which they earn fees upon. URNM’s management expense ratio is 0.75%, which is steep compared to most ETFs, but pretty much in the normal range for a specialist commodity/mining ETF. For the ~11% of URNM that is passed through to SPUT, investors must also incur the cost of SPUT’s management fee, which is 0.64%.

So, in effect, 11% of any money that I choose to invest in URNM will incur an annual management fee cost of 1.39%. This is much too high a fee rate for my liking.

Governance Concerns

As a general rule, I try to avoid investing in companies that are domiciled in/have significant operations in markets that I see as scoring low in terms of transparency or that lack financial and/or political stability. Whilst acknowledging that all metrics have their limitations, I think that Worldwide Governance Indicators (‘WGI’) published by the World Bank can be helpful in terms of identifying countries/regions that do not meet my investment comfort criteria.

URNM’s second largest holding (after Cameco Corp) is NAC Kazatomprom JSC (‘KAP’). KAP is based in Kazakhstan and majority-owned by the Kazakh government. KAP currently represents about 13% of the URNM portfolio. Excluding cash and URNM’s holding in SPUT (physical U308), KAP makes up ~14.6% of the URNM’s portfolio of operating companies.

URNM’s sixth largest holding (or fifth largest excluding SPUT) is CGN Mining Co. Ltd (‘CGN’). CGN is based in China (Hong Kong) and majority-owned by the state-owned China General Nuclear Power Corporation. CGN currently represents about 5% of the URNM portfolio. Excluding cash and URNM’s holding in SPUT (physical U308), CGN makes up ~5.6% of the URNM’s portfolio of operating companies.

Looking beyond KAP, some of URNM’s other holdings also have exposure to assets based in Kazakhstan, notably Cameco’s Inkai uranium JV (Cameco owns 40%, KAP owns 60%). CGN also has assets in Kazakhstan.

The WGI data (Worldwide Governance Indicators, 2024 Update, World Bank - www.govindicators.org , accessed on 10/30/2024) includes six indicators. Kazakhstan scores poorly (less than the 60th percentile) on all six fronts, and is particularly low on Rule of Law (36.79 percentile rank) and Political Stability and Absence of Violence/Terrorism (36.49 percentile rank).

China also scores poorly on five measures, but has a somewhat better rank for Government Effectiveness (73.58 percentile rank). China’s rankings are particularly low on Regulatory Quality (38.68 percentile rank) and Political Stability and Absence of Violence/Terrorism (25.12 percentile rank).

My conclusion from this analysis is that an investment in URNM would lead to an exposure of around 20% being allocated to regions that I would otherwise chose to avoid investing in. In making this statement, I should clarify that I would regard myself as slightly risk averse (for example, I am not particularly drawn to the idea investing in emerging markets).

Push-back On Bullish Arguments

In this section I will set out some of the concerns that crossed my mind when I critically reflected on the bull points supporting the uranium/nuclear thematic.

  • Small Modular Reactors (SMRs) – there is no guarantee that the roll-out of small nuclear plants will be a successful commercial strategy. Without the advantages that SMRs deliver in terms of lower capital costs and quicker build times, governments may be less keen to commit to new nuclear projects.
  • New technology to reduce emissions from other energy sources, or to crack the carbon capture and storage puzzle could dampen demand for new nuclear reactors.
  • Similarly, major advances in battery technology could allow wind and solar to be used for base load power, negating the need for new nuclear capacity.
  • Blowouts in budgets and/or timelines for nuclear reactor projects due to complete over the next few years might make governments cautious about doing more.
  • The above point is not helped by the trends we've seen for ever-increasing complexity in things like obtaining environmental approvals and dealing with indigenous land rights issues
  • AI and data center forecast growth rates for electricity use could be way too optimistic. Advances in cooling technology and data center efficiency seem likely, and these would reduce data center electricity usage.
  • Similarly, advances that allow AI to operate on less compute would be likely to dampen the bullish electricity demand argument.
  • The assumption that the major AI players will look to nuclear power to solve their electricity demand problem is a questionable one. For example, Google has this week agreed to secure as much as 3GW of US hydropower to feed its data centers.
  • On the supply side of the equation, as with nearly all commodities, we know that strong demand will trigger an investment of capital to deliver increased supply.
  • Russia is a large miner of uranium (ranked about 5th to 7th). An end to the Ukraine war might bring Russian uranium back into the market, putting downward pressure on prices.

Sprott and others have made the argument that with nuclear’s share of electricity production sitting at around just 9%, there is a lot of scope for this to increase. Looking back at the historical data, this same argument could have been made for years, and yet we did not see nuclear taking share of the global electricity production market. According to IAEA analysis, the share of nuclear grew rapidly from 1980 to 1990, almost doubling over that period, but has declined since 2000.

That being said, if much of the new global energy production since 2000 has come from developing countries, nuclear may not have been a viable option in those regions. For North America, the nuclear share of electricity production has been stable at around 17% since 2022. Europe is at ~19%, but that has decreased from ~30% in about 2000. I'm assuming that a chunk of the decline is due to Germany turning away from nuclear post-Fukishima.

In their March 2025 URNM presentation, Sprott provide the following slide in support of their uranium bull thesis:

The intention of the chart appears to be to persuade readers that current uranium prices are not high relative to historical uranium prices adjusted for inflation. I would push back on that view a little, but not aggressively. My interpretation of the data is that the current uranium price is broadly in line with the historical average, but only if we factor in previous large price spikes that seem somewhat unlikely to repeat. It should be recognized that the uranium price has traded in a wide range, so the historical average may well not have a lot of predictive power.

Conclusion & Rating

Although I find myself quite persuaded by the positive arguments for uranium, I think that the overall investment case needs to take in to account the downside risks that I have identified in this note.

On balance, at the end of this review, I came away feeling quite bullish about the long-term outlook for uranium/nuclear. However, I am far from convinced that URNM is the best way to play the uranium/nuclear thematic. Whilst some investors may not share my governance concerns regarding URNM’s geographic exposure, the fee leakage from URNM’s investment in Sprott’s physical uranium fund is much harder to ignore.

I will be looking to build a more bespoke exposure to the uranium/nuclear thematic in a way that delivers better governance and fee-leakage outcomes relative to holding URNM. The downside to my approach is that it will involve more time and effort than simply investing in the URNM ETF. Although I can see the appeal of URNM for some investors, it does not strike me as the optimal way to gain exposure to the uranium/nuclear thematic, and I therefore conclude this review with a Hold rating.

data.title: Staying Focused Through Noise
data.description:
In a series of heart-stopping swings, since mid-December, the tech-heavy S&P 500 and Nasdaq are currently up 2.9% and 2.7%, respectively, while the US dollar (as measured by the DXY) has fallen 10% against major trading partners. The more economically sensitive Dow Jones Index is down 2% since December, while the S&P 600 (small-cap companies) is down 10% since the November 2024 peak and down 3.7% since November 2021.

Still, the consensus remains extremely bullish, and FOMO (fear of missing out) is rampant.

Under the indices, the most cyclical segments of the US stock market are in various stages of decline: S&P 500 autos & parts (-31%); home furnishings (-30%); homebuilders (-28%); office REITs (-21%); media (-16%); residential REITs (-12%); retail REITs (-10%); US regional Banks (-11%); and Dow Transports (-11%).

In Canada, the TSX has fared better, up 5.9% since December 6, 2024, helped by a rise in precious metal companies (XGD:CA, +25% since October), while its heavyweight fossil fuel sector (XEG:CA) is down 11.8% since April 2024.

Little covered by financial media is the fact that Treasury bond prices have been rising with much less volatility and principal risk than equities. Not counting interest payments received, Canada’s 3-year Treasury bond is up 9.5% since March 2024, the 5-year bond is up 8% since August 2022, and the 10-year bond is +10% since March 2022.

Not surprisingly, a world full of salespeople promoting equity allocations insists that Treasuries are for dummies and stocks are for the savvy.

Corporate bonds, meanwhile, are priced as if risk-free, with investment-grade bonds yielding less than 1% over similar-dated Treasuries and low-quality “junk” bonds less than 3% - about half the historical yield spread norm.

Conflicts of interest are so endemic that US President Trump is openly promoting financial products and those of his supporters while publicly demanding that the central bank slash interest rates.

With tariff threats and inflation still lingering, the US Federal Reserve and the Bank of Canada are set to maintain policy inertia this month. Their reticence is understandable, but with the labour market weakening and loan delinquencies leaping, rescue efforts will come later than usual this cycle.

A record 11,400 Americans turn 65 every day amid extreme capital risk in their savings.

Approximately 30% of the population belongs to the 55+ age group, who own some 79% of all stocks and equity funds (Deutsche Bank research), now trading at irrationally high valuations—the S&P 500 is priced at a whacky 210% of US GDP. We’ve never seen such madness.

When prices ‘correct’, the pain and suffering will be widespread and difficult to recover from later in life.

I’m tired of pointing out the obvious, and I’m not sure who needs to hear this, but we are living through a wild confluence of factors.

Minimizing capital losses has to be the priority. That requires staying focused on our own financial plan and not succumbing to popular delusions.

Disclosure: No positions

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Li Auto: Jump Onboard To A Long-Term Growth Destination
data.description:
Li Auto (NASDAQ:LI) shares are up around 21% year-to-date, but the long-term trend of electric vehicle adoption will eventually align with a resurgent Chinese consumer.

Introduction to Li Auto Inc.

Li Auto is a leader in the fast-growing Chinese electric vehicle sector. The company has a mission statement of: “Create a Mobile Home, Create Happiness.” The company focuses on producing family SUVs, with plans to expand in the future to a broader range of drivers. The company has recently celebrated its tenth anniversary and is the top-selling brand above the RMB200,000 price range ($27,850). The company has 530 retail stores in 151 cities, 511 service centers, and 2,851 supercharging stations.

China steps in to tackle ‘irregular competition’ in EVs

News this week said that the Chinese authorities were intervening to halt what it calls “irregular competition” in the electric vehicle space. During a State Council meeting chaired by Premier Li Qiang, the government said they would step up price monitoring, regulate the market, and urge companies to innovate at high quality.

Authorities have decided to step in after recent price cuts from BYD intensified a two-year price war and led to criticism. By intervening in this way, the Chinese government is looking to support a diversified sector, rather than watching the largest players outmuscle the competition.

Further support is likely to be coming from the government with comments from the meeting saying that they will take measures to boost domestic consumption and support technological advances.

These measures can help to reassure investors that the Chinese EV sector is not a race to the bottom, and the second half of 2025 could see new measures incoming to support manufacturers.

Statistics support a long-term growth play in EVs

China continues to lead the way in electric vehicle adoption, with sales rising 28% in June to 1.11 million units, or 60% of world sales. Europe is also growing with demand rising 23% to 390,000 units, according to the latest data.

Another interesting point to note is that Tesla has been losing some ground in the Chinese market, but Europeans are also embracing Chinese models. Data in June saw a 40% drop in Tesla sales in the Eurozone, with Chinese manufacturers selling 65,808 cars and doubling their market share to 5.9%.

Global sales of EVs hit 9.1 million in the first half of 2025 and government subsidies should support growth in the second half. A focus on price gouging should also see the market regulate to support a wider share of EV companies.

It was a disappointing month for Li Auto, with deliveries of 36,279 vehicles in June, down 24% year-over-year and 11.2% lower than May. However, this was driven by the price war of high-volume, low-cost competitors. Li produces vehicles aimed at high-end purchases and family buyers, which has likely been a headwind due to consumer stress. The recent slowdown came after a 20% sales increase in the previous month. Li has also guided slower deliveries in H2 due to a system upgrade.

Li is releasing its i6 SUV in September, which is slated to compete with the Mercedes-Benz GLC, BMW X3, and Audi Q5.

I believe that the sector will mature as the Chinese consumer recovers from a very cautious period. The government intervention to support the broader sector and can boost the outlook for Li.

More on the Chinese consumer

The Chinese economy had a disappointing recovery from the lockdown era and that has been a headwind for domestic consumption. Alongside the property sector’s problems, the country has also seen overseas investment slowing.

Data has shown that the Chinese consumer has developed a “purpose-driven consumption,” where big-ticket items have been shunned in favor of necessary spending. The trend has been a headwind for other sectors, such as luxury goods, and the government has also created measures to support household appliance sales.

This is also a reason why lower-end car sales have picked up, and higher-end models such as those by Li, have seen sales pressures. There have been some green shoots of recovery in the Chinese consumer lately and I also believe that the long-term trend will support high-end consumption again.

A look at Li’s finances and valuation

Li currently has a forward price/sales ratio of 0.71x, according to Seeking Alpha data. That is lower than the sector median of 1.24x. Price/cash flow is also in line with the broader sector at 10.71x. Li’s capital structure is sound with total debt of $2.25 billion, but $15.25 billion in cash.

Vehicle sales in the latest first quarter results were RMB24.7 billion (US$3.4 billion). That was an increase of 1.8% from RMB24.3 billion over the year, but a decrease of 42.1% from RMB42.6 billion in the fourth quarter of 2024.

Margins on vehicles were 19.8% in the first quarter of 2025, compared to 19.3% in the first quarter of 2024 and 19.7% in the fourth quarter of 2024. Resilient margins are the reason that Bank of America and Morgan Stanley analysts remain bullish on the stock.

Risks to the investment thesis

One risk to the investment thesis would be oversupply in the EV sector, where government subsidies have led to the rise of around 100 different EV manufacturers. However, the price war and competition are likely to see many of those brands disappear. Li’s strong cash position will allow the company to ride out the storm in the near term.

The other issue would be the recent year-on-year decline in sales, but as I noted, the price war benefits that have worked for Li’s competitors will no longer be tolerated by authorities.

The annual revenue picture shows strong growth for the company and it is now delivering profitability with margin stability. That puts the company in a good place to enhance its technology and processes ahead of an inevitable rebound in the Chinese economy and its consumers.

Conclusion

Li Auto suffered a yearly decline in sales and a monthly dip of -11% in the most recent data, but I believe that the strained Chinese consumer has gravitated to cheaper models. That was also driven by price cuts and the Chinese government is now intervening to ensure a more sustainable sector. These trends, alongside the long-term adoption cycle in China, make Li Auto a good investment at these levels.

data.title: Tariffs' Unseen Impact: First Time Since 2017
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Tariffs are once again dominating headlines as August 1st approaches. The imminent deadline demonstrates the Trump administration's firm stance, and several trading partners, such as the EU, Japan, South Korea, Canada, Mexico, and many others, could be impacted.

While tariffs bring market volatility, the purpose of this article is to provide a general overview of their effects. While the impacts may have begun to affect inflation, the fact is that tariffs have boosted government revenues, even contributing to a fiscal surplus in June, something not seen since 2017.

Context

The year 2025 will be remembered in the books as the year of tariffs. "Liberation Day" on April 2nd marked the Trump administration's announcement of tariffs for several trading partners, and fears about the impacts on the economy and global trade caused the market to plummet.

After a 90-day lull in tariff negotiations, the S&P 500 recovered and once again reached an all-time high. The initial deadline for all countries to negotiate an agreement was July 9th, but it was extended to August 1st. Despite these postponements, tariffs are once again a hot topic and the risks increase again.

Tariff Changes Since April

From now on, I will highlight what investors should monitor closely, as well as explore in depth the latest developments in tariffs and their impact on trade and investments.

The European Union was threatened with 30% tariffs, and Canada was threatened with 35% tariffs. Additionally, fourteen Asian countries, including Japan and Korea, have received letters informing them that tariffs will be between 25% and 40%. The table below compares tariffs from Liberation Day in April to the new deadline of August 1.

Country/Bloc Liberation Day August 1 (Deadline)
EU 20% tariffs (existing 25% steel/aluminum tariffs) 30% tariffs
Japan 24% tariffs 25% tariffs
South Korea 25% tariffs 25% tariffs
Canada 25% tariffs, except USMCA products 35% tariffs

The list is much longer. Brazil, for example, which I wrote about in more detail in a recent article. The figure below is intended to illustrate the tariff changes from liberation day in April to August 1st.

Something that caught my attention is the difference in deadlines. After the day of liberation, the Trump administration announced a 90-day truce for negotiations. The deadline was supposed to end on July 9th, but it's interesting that the additional deadline is now August 1st, giving only 23 days of negotiations.

This demonstrates a tougher stance from the Trump administration and the impression that this is a genuine ultimatum. But after all, what is the real impact of tariff policy on the American economy?

Tariffs and Their Impact on the Economy

As incredible as it may seem, tariffs are reaching unimaginable levels. For reference, tariff revenue was $7.9 billion in January, reaching more than $15 billion in April. Let's see the chart below.

This year alone, tariffs have generated over $100 billion in customs duties for the US, and in June alone, the US received over $27 billion in tariffs (+301% y/y).

But the most unexpected positive impact, in my view, occurred in the fiscal surplus in June. The 7% annual increase in revenue (driven by tariffs) outpaced expenditures, which increased by 6%. The last recorded surplus was in June 2017.

As a result, tariffs now represent 4.9% of US tax receipts, as we can see in the figure below. We've seen the benefits of tariffs, but what about the risks? We'll analyze the impact on inflation in the next chapter.

Tariff Risks For The Economy

There's nothing better than analyzing inflation with the latest CPI data. The CPI rose to 2.7% year-on-year, the highest level since February. However, the Core CPI was 2.9% below the consensus of 3%.

It's clear that tariffs have begun to have some effect on the prices of core goods, such as footwear, toys, clothing, and furniture. The drop in auto prices helped mitigate the impact.

This data appears mixed, but it seems to me that the revenue benefits of tariffs appear to outweigh the impacts on inflation.

Another risk lies in Americans' concerns. If the public fears that prices will rise due to tariffs (as the survey below suggests), this could accelerate consumption and put pressure on inflation.

What To Do With Your Investments?

This article aims to clarify the implications of tariffs on the American economy. However, is it worth changing your investment philosophy because of this? The answer is no, even if supported by historical evidence.

This isn't the first time the market has been stressed by tariffs, but since then, prices have recovered and even surpassed expectations. Furthermore, it seems to me that the positive effect of tariffs on revenues more than offsets the impact on inflation, at least for now. Based on this analysis, I reiterate my positive outlook for the S&P 500 with a target of 7,095 points.

The Bottom Line

After causing significant volatility in the first half of the year, tariffs are back in the spotlight following a series of announcements and letters from the Trump administration to various trading partners.

The postponement of the July 9 deadline to just August 1 demonstrates the U.S. government's firmness, a kind of ultimatum. The fact is that tariffs are contributing significantly to revenue, given the fiscal surplus achieved in June.

Tariffs appear to be responsible for the slight acceleration in the CPI; however, the benefits to government revenues appear to more than offset the risks, at least for now. This analysis corroborates my positive view on the S&P 500.

data.title: AI Infrastructure Company WhiteFiber Begins IPO Rollout
data.description:
WhiteFiber Is Growing Quickly And Profitably

WhiteFiber, Inc. (WYFI) has filed to raise $100 million in an IPO of its ordinary shares, according to SEC S-1 registration information.

WYFI provides artificial intelligence infrastructure solutions for businesses.

The company is growing revenue quickly and generating operating profit, but has limited operating history and customer and data center concentration.

I’ll provide a final opinion when we get more IPO details from management.

What Does WhiteFiber Do?

New York, NY-based WhiteFiber, Inc. was founded as a subsidiary of Bit Digital (BTBT) to develop AI-oriented infrastructure capabilities from its Montreal Tier-3 high performance computing data center in Montreal, Canada.

Management is led by Chief Executive Officer Mr. Sam Tabar, who has been with the firm since February 2025 and is also CEO of Bit Digital, which is the primary shareholder of the firm, and has extensive business experience in various disciplines.

The company's primary offerings include:

  • Cloud services - AI GPU services

  • Colocation services

The firm offers its services through its Montreal data center and has recently secured the rights for a new data center site in a suburb of Montreal.

In addition, it intends to retrofit a recently purchased facility in North Carolina to create an HPC (High Performance Computing) data center.

WhiteFiber has booked fair market value investment of $221 million as of March 31, 2025 from parent firm Bit Digital.

What Is WhiteFiber’s Market?

According to a 2025 market research report by Precedence Research, the US market for artificial intelligence products of all types is an estimated $174 billion in 2025 and is expected to exceed $851 billion by 2034.

This represents a projected very strong CAGR of 19.3% from 2025 to 2034.

The primary factors driving this expected growth are strong demand from businesses to automate and improve their internal processes and from consumers for general productivity gains.

Also, the chart below shows the expected trajectory of the industry through 2034:

Major competitive vendors or industry players include the following firms:

  • Digital Realty (DNR)

  • Equinix (EQIX)

  • NTT (OTCPK:NTTYY)

  • Cyrus One

  • STACK Infrastructure

  • Aligned Data Centers

  • Iron Mountain (IRM)

  • Various private operators in the U.S.

  • CoreWeave (CRWV)

  • Crusoe Energy

  • Nebius (NBIS)

  • Lambda Labs

The company seeks clients interested in its large-scale GPU and HPC compute capacity via its direct sales team.

As of the end of 2024, 96% of its revenue came from its Cloud services, as the pie chart shows below:

I’ve prepared a thumbnail SWOT analysis for WhiteFiber:

Strengths

  • Vertically integrated business model, partnerships with major equipment providers, focus on Tier-3 data centers

Weaknesses

  • Data center and customer concentration, limited operating history, high CapEx requirements

Opportunities

  • Rapid growth in AI markets, existing site expansion potential, proprietary software for GPU management

Threats

  • Intense competition from major industry players, supply chain dependence

WhiteFiber’s Recent Financial Results

The firm’s recent financial performance is summarized as shown below:

  • Quickly growing topline revenue
