The company has generated free cash flow for the twelve months ended March 31, 2025, of a whopping negative ($110 million).

WhiteFiber’s IPO Plan

WYFI has filed to sell $100 million worth of its ordinary shares, although the final figure could be higher.

No possibly new shareholders have indicated an interest in acquiring additional shares in the first publicly available prospectus.

Company, shareholder and executive lock-ups will be for the usual 180 days after the effective date of the IPO prospectus.

Upon completion of the IPO, its parent firm, Bit Digital, will hold 80% of the voting power of the public entity, so the company will be a ‘controlled company’ according to Nasdaq’s rules.

According to WhiteFiber’s most recent regulatory filing, it plans to use the net proceeds as follows:

We expect that we will use the net proceeds from this offering as follows: partially fund the lease or purchase of additional property on which to build additional WhiteFiber data centers; to construct those facilities; to enter into additional energy service agreements for each additional site; to purchase GPUs, servers and other AI equipment; for potential acquisitions, partnerships and joint ventures; research and development, and to fund our working capital and general corporate purposes.

(Source - SEC)

The firm’s presentation of the company roadshow is not available yet.

Pertaining to potential legal exposure, leadership said the company is not currently a party to any legal action that would have a material adverse effect on its business or financial condition.

Listed underwriters of the IPO are B. Riley Securities and Needham & Company.

WhiteFiber Is Growing Quickly And Slightly Profitably

WhiteFiber is seeking US public capital market funding for its data center growth plans and operating requirements.

WYFI’s financials have shown sharply growing topline revenue, growing gross profit and gross margin, increasing operating profit but a swing to cash used in operations.

Free cash flow for the twelve months ended March 31, 2025, was a whopping negative ($110 million), as the company built out its compute capabilities.

General & Administrative expenses as a percentage of total revenue has risen as revenue has grown and its General & Administrative efficiency multiple fell to 2.0x.

The firm currently plans to pay no dividends and will likely retain any future earnings and funds for its growth and working capital needs.

WYFI’s recent capital spending history shows it has spent heavily on capital expenditures.

Of note is that as of January 1, 2025, the company changed its estimated useful life of its cloud service equipment from three to five years, reducing depreciation expense as a result.

This is somewhat controversial, as there is substantial disagreement among industry observers about the true useful life of current GPUs as the supplier industry moves quickly ahead on innovation, potentially reducing the useful life of this equipment in the process.

The firm’s growth strategy features building or outfitting additional data center locations to provide its services and accessing additional capital to do so.

The market opportunity for providing AI compute capabilities is large and expected to grow at a high rate of growth in the coming years, so the company has strong industry growth dynamics in its favor.

However, it faces significant competition from deep-pocketed industry participants.

Risks to the company’s outlook as a public firm include its ‘smaller reporting company’ and ‘emerging growth company’ status, which enables management to produce materially less information for shareholders.

Also, the company has data center concentration risks and fewer than 15 customers as of May 30, 2025.

When we gain more information from management about the IPO’s pricing and valuation assumptions, I’ll provide a final opinion.

Expected IPO Pricing Date: To be announced

data.title: Hershey: Headwinds Might Last Longer Than Initially Expected (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
The Hershey Company (NYSE:HSY) is one of those businesses that rarely come at a discount. It's a high-quality business operating in a relatively predictable industry where its strong brands create an almost impossible-to-replicate competitive advantage.

With that in mind, more often than not, long-term investors would be better off gradually increasing their positions in HSY, especially when the stock falls sharply due to short-term headwinds.

Although the current period fits these criteria, I am very skeptical that the stock could deliver satisfactory returns through the rest of 2025.

The reason why I say that is because the current headwinds for profitability are likely to last for longer than initially expected, and Hershey's return to prior levels of profitability would not be smooth sailing.

With that in mind, the share price levels of $200 and above might not be achieved for quite some time, and even if they are, it's unlikely that they will be sustained for long.

Lasting Headwinds

As a starting point, back in early 2025, it appeared as if cocoa prices were heading south, and this significantly increased the probability of HSY's margins rebounding in FY 2026. Since then, however, we have witnessed a sharp increase in May, and we are now back to the recent lows from late 2024. While this might prompt some investors to speculate on prices continuing to fall, the reality is that more volatility is to be expected.

In the meantime, HSY's gross margins collapsed in Q1 of 2025 and are now at multi-year lows. Again, that was mostly anticipated and in line with the company's FY 2025 guidance.

During the last earnings release, HSY's management also indicated that gross margin pressures will only intensify from here on.

Based on our commodity hedges for the balance of the year, cost pressure from cocoa will build each quarter. We expect second quarter gross margin compression to be greater than in Q1. There is no change to our underlying full-year gross margin outlook of down 650 to 700 basis points, including the impacts of incremental tariffs in Q2.

Source: HSY Q1 2025 Prepared Remarks

On the cost side, this does not deviate from the 2025 outlook, and management still expects earnings to fall by almost 50%.

This drop in earnings has resulted in HSY now trading at a forward P/E ratio of almost 30 - a multiple that at a first glance does not seem prohibitively high.

However, HSY has traded at multiples of 30 and above only a handful of times in 35 years. These periods were mostly during times when the equity market collapsed or the company's earnings were temporarily under pressure.

What the graph above illustrates is that the market is once again convinced that the earnings drop will be only temporary. The problem is that a recovery is likely to take longer than expected, which could lead to some pressure through the rest of 2025.

How Quickly Can Earnings Rebound?

My main concern about HSY's profitability going forward has not been the cocoa prices, but rather the company's ability to pass the higher input costs onto the consumer through price increases.

The area that I am more concerned about is the company's ability to negate the impact of higher costs through pricing.

(...) it might be too soon for yet another cycle of price increases, without the company experiencing a more meaningful drop in volumes.

Source: Seeking Alpha

When the company reported its Q1 2025 results, it appeared as if these concerns had materialized, as the impact on volume/mix on net sales was around a negative 15%, while the effect of pricing was only 2%.

HSY's management made clear that the volume headwinds were due to inventory increases, a later Easter season, and fewer shipping days.

Volume declined about 15 points, reflecting the lap of planned inventory increases ahead of our ERP system implementation in the prior year period, a later Easter season in 2025, and two fewer shipping days. As mentioned last quarter, the Easter and ERP laps will reverse, and we therefore expect total Company net sales to increase more than 20% in Q2.

Source: HSY Q1 2025 Prepared Remarks

These one-off items would make it much harder to isolate the impact of higher pricing on volumes in the first half of 2025, and this might give investors some confidence that HSY would be able to quickly mitigate the impact of higher input costs through increased pricing.

But given the very recent price increases in the 2022–23 period and the record-low consumer confidence, I remain skeptical that volumes will be as stable as most investors expect. Just recently, we saw Hershey's main peer, Mondelez (MDLZ), reporting a significant drop in volumes as the company once again stepped up its pricing initiatives.

On top of all that, Hershey's management also anticipates a significant increase in marketing spend through the rest of 2025, which will put even more pressure on margins.

And we also expect SG&A dollars will be up meaningfully. And part of that is last year, we didn't spend much on marketing and so on as we came out of the ERP transformation. So you can expect that this year, we'll have high teens year-over-year SG&A growth in the second quarter, just reflecting that bigger lap.

Source: HSY Q1 2025 Earnings Transcript

This would likely be done in order to support the pricing initiatives, but consumer fatigue could easily overshadow the impact on Hershey's topline.

Conclusion

Hershey trades at a significant discount to its historical averages, but that doesn't mean that a reversal would arrive anytime soon. Although the business would inevitably return to its prior highs, there is a bumpy road ahead. On top of the unpredictable nature of commodity prices, it appears highly likely that the market might be overestimating the company's ability to further increase prices without sacrificing volumes. For these reasons, I believe that a Hold rating is now more appropriate for HSY as we progress through the rest of 2025.
